reasoning: The Lean formalization uses `Real.logb 10 x`, which Mathlib defines for all real x via `logb b x = log|x|/log b` (see `logb_neg_eq_logb` in Mathlib/Analysis/SpecialFunctions/Log/Base.lean). The informal statement's "log(x)" implicitly requires x > 0 for the expression to be meaningful. By omitting this implicit positivity premise and quantifying over all reals, the Lean lemma `in_interval_of_log_inequality` claims the implication for negative x as well. Concrete counterexample: x = -10⁻³ gives `logb 10 x = -3`, so `-logb 10 x = 3` which satisfies `2.3 < 3 < 5.4`, yet x ∉ (10⁻⁵·⁴, 10⁻²·³) (a positive interval). So the Lean lemma direction is strictly stronger than (and actually false under) the informal claim, which under standard math reading is true. The axiom direction is fine since interval membership forces x > 0, but the lemma direction drops the implicit hypothesis that log(x) be defined.
